summary:
The Chimú Empire (c. 900–1470 AD) was the largest and most sophisticated pre-Inca state on the Pacific coast of South America, controlling a coastal empire stretching over 1,000 kilometers from northern Peru through Ecuador from its capital [[event:2315|Chan Chan]] — the largest adobe city ever built. The Chimú civilization developed from the earlier Moche culture, inheriting and expanding the elaborate irrigation systems, metalworking traditions, and hierarchical social organization of its Moche predecessors, constructing vast canal systems that brought water from Andean rivers to the coastal desert and enabled intensive irrigated agriculture in one of the driest environments on Earth.

Chimú metalworking achieved the highest technical level of any pre-Columbian culture, producing exquisite gold and silver objects of extraordinary craftsmanship — vessels, masks, headdresses, and jewelry — using sophisticated techniques including alloying, soldering, repoussé, and gilding. The Chimú political system was highly centralized, with a divine king (Sapa Chimu) at the apex of a rigidly stratified society. The empire was conquered by the [[event:99|Inca Empire]] under Pachacuti's son Tupac Inca Yupanqui around 1470, in a campaign that took several years because the Inca cut the Chimú's irrigation canals — rendering the coastal desert agriculture impossible and forcing submission. The Inca subsequently incorporated Chimú administrative practices, craft specialists, and luxury goods traditions into their expanding empire, making Chimú a crucial cultural bridge between coastal and Andean Peruvian traditions.
